content.problemStatement:
Chef is going to leave his home town and shift to a new city. The new city contains exactly **N** houses. There are roads connecting the houses in such a way that there is exactly one way to go from a house to any other house. Length of each of the roads is known to Chef.



Chief is doing a lot of research before shifting to the new city. He wants to know how many pairs of houses (a,b) are there, such that if we multiply length of all the roads on the path from a to b, the product will be divisible by **M**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers N and M, where N denotes the number of houses and M is the divisor. Each of the next (N-1) lines contains a triplet of numbers (a, b, c) which denotes that the length of the road between houses a and b is c.
content.outputFormat: The number of pairs of houses (a, b) such that if we multiply the length of all the roads on the path from a to b, the product will be divisible by M.

sampleTestCases.explanation: If we multiply the lengths of the roads on the path between any pair of the vertices, it will be divisible by 2.
If we multiply the lengths of the roads on the path between any pair of the vertices, it will be divisible by 3.
If we multiply the lengths of the roads on the path between any pair of the vertices, it will be divisible by 5.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a depth-first search (DFS) approach to traverse the graph and calculate the product of the road lengths for each pair of houses. We can use a recursive function to perform the DFS and keep track of the current product. If the product is divisible by M, we increment the count of pairs.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * (N-1)) — because in the worst case, we need to perform DFS from each node to every other node.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — because we need to store the adjacency list representation of the graph.
